planting.note: Plant in fall for fringed late-spring blooms.

directions: Set bulbs about 6 inches deep in a sunny, well-drained spot. Ivory-and-rose petals rimmed with delicate crystalline fringe open in late spring. The unusual edging shows best when plants are grouped where they can be viewed closely.

planting.note: Autumn planting suits this fringed tulip.

directions: Plant bulbs 6 inches deep in fertile ground with sharp drainage. Pure white petals fringed with fine, feathery edges open in late spring on tall stems. Let foliage yellow completely before removing to build the bulb.

planting.note: Plant in fall for late-spring fringed color.

directions: Set bulbs about 6 inches deep in sun and freely draining soil. Violet-purple petals edged with pale, crystalline fringe open in late spring. Grouping the bulbs highlights the frosted margins that give this tulip its charm.

planting.note: Plant in fall for long-lasting late-spring blooms.

directions: Plant bulbs about 6 inches deep in a sunny bed with good drainage. Blended salmon-orange and green petals with a green feathered stripe open in late spring and last a long time. The green markings are the signature of the Viridiflora group.

planting.note: Set bulbs in autumn for late-spring flowers.

directions: Bury bulbs 6 inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil. Soft rose-pink petals with a broad green central stripe open in late spring on strong stems. Long-lasting in the garden and vase; let leaves ripen before cutting back.

planting.note: Plant in fall for compact late-spring color.

directions: Plant bulbs about 5 inches deep in a bright, sharply drained spot. Soft pink petals striped with green sit above cream-edged foliage, opening in late spring. Its short stature suits containers and border fronts.

planting.note: Plant in fall for flamboyant late-spring blooms.

directions: Set bulbs about 6 inches deep in a sheltered, sunny bed. Ruffled and twisted petals streaked red and white with green feathering open in late spring. Site out of strong wind, as the large parrot flowers are heavy.

planting.note: Autumn planting suits this ruffled parrot type.

directions: Plant bulbs 6 inches deep in fertile soil with good drainage. Fringed, twisting golden-yellow petals with a thin red edge open in late spring. Shelter from wind protects the large, showy flowers.

planting.note: Plant in fall for late-spring parrot blooms.

directions: Bury bulbs about 6 inches deep in a sheltered, well-drained bed. Ruffled petals blend apricot, cream, and rose with feathers of green in late spring. Protect the heavy, fringed flowers from wind and driving rain.

planting.note: Set bulbs in autumn for late-spring color.

directions: Plant bulbs 6 inches deep in a sunny, sheltered spot with sharp drainage. Large ruffled petals in violet-blue with a bronze reverse open in late spring. Group the bulbs and shield them from wind to keep the big flowers upright.

planting.note: Plant in fall for peony-like late-spring flowers.

directions: Set bulbs about 6 inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil. Full double white blooms boldly striped with red, over green-and-white variegated leaves, open in late spring. Shelter the heavy peony-style flowers from wind and rain.

planting.note: Autumn planting suits this double late tulip.

directions: Plant bulbs 6 inches deep in rich soil with good drainage. Dense double flowers in deep maroon-red open in late spring, resembling small peonies. Give them a spot sheltered from wind so the full heads stay upright.

planting.note: Plant in fall for double late blooms.

directions: Set bulbs about 6 inches deep in a sunny, well-drained bed. Double orange blooms flushed with purple and green markings open in late spring, a sport of Princess Irene. Shelter from wind and let foliage ripen after flowering.

planting.note: Plant in fall for full late-spring flowers.

directions: Plant bulbs 6 inches deep in fertile, freely draining soil. Densely petaled double blooms in soft lilac-purple open in late spring. Protect the peony-like flowers from wind and heavy rain to keep them standing.

planting.note: Plant in fall for very early, water-lily-like flowers.

directions: Set bulbs about 5 inches deep in a sunny, sharply drained spot. Golden-yellow petals with red exteriors open flat like water lilies in early spring above mottled leaves. Low and early, it naturalizes well in rock gardens.

planting.note: Plant in fall for early dwarf blooms.

directions: Plant bulbs about 5 inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil. Yellow petals flamed carmine-red on the outside open wide in early spring over purple-mottled foliage. Ideal for rock gardens and the fronts of beds.

planting.note: Plant in fall for early to mid-spring flowers.

directions: Set bulbs about 6 inches deep in fertile, well-drained ground. Large flowers open pale yellow with a deep yellow base and fade to creamy white as they age. This Fosteriana blooms early on strong stems and returns reliably.

planting.note: Plant in fall for early spring flowers over patterned leaves.

directions: Plant bulbs about 5 inches deep in a sunny, sharply drained bed. Apricot-yellow petals edged and flushed with bronze-red open in early to mid spring above mottled, purple-streaked foliage. Compact and long-lived, it suits rockeries and edges.

planting.note: Plant in fall for compact early-spring blooms.

directions: Set bulbs about 5 inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil. Bright red petals broadly edged in white open in early spring over maroon-mottled leaves. Its short stature makes it a strong choice for pots and rock gardens.

planting.note: Plant in fall in a warm, well-drained spot; it prefers mild-winter regions.

directions: Set bulbs about 4 inches deep in sharply drained, gritty soil in a sunny, sheltered site. Fragrant lilac-pink flowers with a bright yellow center open in mid spring. This Cretan species spreads by stolons and thrives with a hot, dry summer rest.

planting.note: Plant in fall in a sunny, freely draining bed.

directions: Set bulbs about 5 inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun. The unusual flowers have very long, narrow, twisted petals in yellow flushed with red, opening in mid to late spring. Keep the soil dry through summer dormancy for best return.

planting.note: Plant in fall in a hot, sharply drained rock garden position.

directions: Set the small bulbs about 3 inches deep in very gritty, free-draining soil. Star-shaped pink to magenta flowers with a yellow center open in early spring on very short stems. This tiny species is ideal for troughs and rock gardens with a dry summer bake.

planting.note: Plant in fall in a sunny, sharply drained spot.

directions: Set bulbs about 4 inches deep in gritty, free-draining soil in full sun. Brilliant scarlet-red flowers with a dark base open in mid spring above narrow, wavy-edged leaves. This low species needs a hot, dry summer rest to persist and multiply.

planting.note: Set bulbs from early to mid autumn while the soil is still workable so roots establish before hard frost.

directions: Plant bulbs about three times their own depth, roughly six inches down, in fertile well-drained ground. This early vigorous variety carries a yellow perianth around a bold orange cup and naturalizes freely. Leave the leaves standing for at least six weeks after the blooms fade so the bulb can restock for next spring.

planting.note: Plant in autumn before the ground chills so bulbs root out ahead of winter.

directions: Tuck bulbs six inches deep and about five inches apart into loosened soil. This all-golden, weather-resistant selection shows strong resistance to basal rot and holds up well in beds and grass. Deadhead spent flowers but let the foliage yellow naturally before removing it.

planting.note: Set out in mid autumn for spring flowering.

directions: Bury bulbs pointed end up at about six inches deep in a sunny, well-drained spot. Ceylon opens a clear golden perianth around a flat, sun-fast orange cup on sturdy stems that resist wind and rain. Enjoy the long-lasting blooms, then leave the leaves intact until they collapse on their own.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn a few weeks before the first hard freeze.

directions: Plant these large bulbs six inches deep and give them a little extra elbow room for their broad flowers. Gigantic Star produces oversized, sweetly fragrant golden blooms that make excellent long-stemmed cut flowers. Cut for the vase just as the buds break color and let garden clumps keep their foliage until it fades.

planting.note: Set bulbs out in autumn while the soil remains open.

directions: Dig bulbs in about six inches deep into loose, fertile soil. Accent pairs a snow-white perianth with a large, ruffled coral-pink cup that deepens as it matures. Feed lightly in early spring and allow the leaves to ripen fully before tidying up.

planting.note: Plant in mid autumn for reliable spring color.

directions: Settle bulbs six inches deep and about five inches apart in well-drained ground. Passionale opens white petals framing a soft pink trumpet-shaped cup that ages to a rosy tone. Deadhead as flowers pass and keep the foliage until it yellows to build next year's display.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn ahead of the first hard frost.

directions: Plant bulbs roughly six inches deep in a sunny bed or naturalized drift. This vigorous old favorite carries creamy petals around a soft orange cup and multiplies readily over the years. Let the clumps thicken undisturbed, dividing only when bloom starts to thin.

planting.note: Set bulbs out in autumn for spring bloom.

directions: Plant bulbs six inches deep in fertile, freely draining soil. Kissproof shows a warm apricot-yellow perianth around a bold orange cup for a rich, sunset-toned effect. Remove faded blooms and leave the foliage to die back naturally before cleanup.

planting.note: Plant in autumn while soil is still workable.

directions: Set bulbs about six inches deep in loosened, well-drained ground. Delibes displays a yellow perianth with a broad orange cup edged in a deeper rim, strong for both borders and cutting. Deadhead spent flowers and hold the leaves until they yellow to feed next year's bulb.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn for late-spring flowering.

directions: Plant bulbs six inches deep where their scent can be enjoyed near a path or doorway. As the name promises, the white flowers with pink-flushed cups carry a genuine rose-like fragrance rare among daffodils. Cut a few stems for the vase and let the rest ripen their leaves before you clear them.

planting.note: Plant in early to mid autumn for early spring bloom.

directions: Plant bulbs six inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil. Golden Harvest is a classic large all-yellow trumpet that blooms early and forces well for indoor pots. After flowering, let the foliage stand until it dies back to recharge the bulb.

planting.note: Set the large bulbs out in autumn before the ground freezes.

directions: Plant these big bulbs about six inches deep and slightly farther apart to suit their size. Unsurpassable is prized for enormous golden trumpet flowers on tall, strong stems. Support may help in exposed sites, and the foliage should be left to yellow fully after bloom.

planting.note: Plant in early autumn as this is one of the earliest daffodils to flower.

directions: Plant bulbs six inches deep in a sheltered, sunny spot to protect the very early blooms. Famous for opening its golden trumpets in late winter, sometimes as early as January in mild areas, it signals the start of the season. Enjoy the pioneering flowers and leave the leaves standing to feed the bulb afterward.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn ahead of hard frost.

directions: Dig bulbs in about six inches deep in fertile, freely draining soil. Marieke carries a large, deep-golden trumpet on a robust stem and stands up well to spring weather. Cut for bold arrangements or leave in the ground, allowing foliage to ripen before removal.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for early to mid spring flowering.

directions: Plant bulbs six inches deep in a sunny, well-drained bed. Las Vegas is a large bicolor trumpet with a white perianth and a yellow trumpet that pales as it ages. It naturalizes strongly and makes a fine cut flower; leave the foliage to die back on its own.

planting.note: Plant in autumn near the front of a border or in a rock garden.

directions: Plant these small bulbs about four inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil. Topolino is a dainty early dwarf trumpet with a pale creamy perianth and a soft lemon trumpet. Ideal for rockeries and troughs, it should keep its slim leaves until they wither naturally.

planting.note: Plant in autumn at the front of beds, in pots, or in short grass.

directions: Set bulbs three to four inches deep in well-drained soil or a container mix. Little Gem is one of the earliest miniature trumpets, all golden-yellow and perfectly proportioned. Its small stature suits rock gardens and pots; let the leaves fade before tidying.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for mid to late spring bloom.

directions: Plant these smaller bulbs about four inches deep in gritty, well-drained ground. Segovia is a charming miniature with flat white petals and a tiny lemon-yellow cup that fades to cream. Well suited to rock gardens and the front of borders, it should keep its foliage until it yellows.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for a late-season display.

directions: Plant bulbs about six inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil. Merlin pairs a broad white perianth with a small, frilled yellow cup rimmed in vivid red. It holds its color well in sun and makes a crisp cut flower; leave foliage to ripen after flowering.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn before the soil freezes.

directions: Set bulbs six inches deep in a sunny, freely draining bed. Birma shows a yellow perianth around a small, flat cup of glowing orange-red for a bright two-tone look. A reliable naturalizer, it should keep its leaves until they die back to build next year's bloom.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for full double blooms in spring.

directions: Plant bulbs six inches deep in rich, well-drained soil sheltered from strong wind. Golden Ducat is a fully double, all-yellow sport of King Alfred with peony-like flowers. The heavy heads can flop in rain, so give some support and leave the foliage to ripen after bloom.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for late-spring double flowers.

directions: Plant bulbs six inches deep in fertile ground with good drainage. Acropolis bears large white double flowers studded with segments of orange-red for a bold camellia-like effect. Site it out of harsh wind, and let the leaves die down naturally once blooming ends.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for spring bloom.

directions: Plant bulbs about six inches deep in well-drained soil. White Marvel is a triandrus-type double producing several pure white, softly doubled flowers per stem. It naturalizes well and works in pots; leave the foliage standing until it fades.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for large double flowers in mid to late spring.

directions: Plant bulbs six inches deep in rich, freely draining soil out of strong wind. Obdam opens creamy buds that mature to fully double, gardenia-like ivory-white blooms. The large heads benefit from shelter or staking, and the foliage should ripen before removal.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for late-spring double blooms.

directions: Set bulbs six inches deep in fertile, well-drained ground with some wind protection. Delnashaugh bears large double flowers of white petals interleaved with soft apricot-pink segments. Give the substantial heads shelter or support, and let the leaves fade naturally after bloom.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in mild climates; can be forced indoors in colder regions.

directions: Plant bulbs about five inches deep in warm, well-drained soil, or pot up for indoor forcing where winters are cold. Erlicheer carries clusters of small, double cream-and-yellow florets with a rich, sweet perfume. It thrives in mild-winter gardens; let the foliage ripen fully after flowering.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for nodding bells in mid to late spring.

directions: Plant bulbs about five inches deep in well-drained soil in sun or light shade. Liberty Bells hangs two or more nodding, soft lemon-yellow bell flowers with swept-back petals per stem. It naturalizes gently and suits pots; keep the slim foliage until it dies back.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for late-spring flowering.

directions: Plant bulbs five inches deep in fertile, moisture-retentive but well-drained soil. Lemon Drops dangles several graceful flowers per stem with pale ivory petals and lemon cups. It appreciates a little afternoon shade; let the leaves ripen fully before you remove them.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for fragrant late-spring clusters.

directions: Plant bulbs about four inches deep in well-drained ground in sun or dappled light. Petrel carries several small, fragrant, pure white nodding flowers on each slender stem. Excellent for naturalizing and containers, it should keep its foliage until it yellows naturally.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for early spring bloom at the front of a bed.

directions: Plant these small bulbs about four inches deep in well-drained soil. Jack Snipe is a sturdy dwarf with creamy-white swept-back petals and a short lemon-yellow cup. It naturalizes reliably in rock gardens and short grass; leave the leaves to ripen before cleanup.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for early to mid spring flowers.

directions: Plant bulbs about four inches deep in freely draining soil in sun or light shade. Jenny opens with a lemon cup that fades to creamy white, matching the reflexed white petals. Graceful for pots and borders, it should keep its foliage until it withers.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for early spring blooms.

directions: Plant bulbs about four inches deep in well-drained ground. Dove Wings shows white swept-back petals around a slender pale-yellow trumpet that softens to cream. A dependable naturalizer for borders and grass, it should keep its leaves until they die back.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for mid-spring blooms in a rock garden or border edge.

directions: Plant these small bulbs about four inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil. Beryl is a dainty old cultivar with pale primrose petals and a small, flat orange cup. Well suited to rockeries and troughs, it should hold its slim foliage until it yellows.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for fragrant mid-spring flowers.

directions: Plant bulbs about five inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun. Trevithian carries two or three richly fragrant, clear lemon-yellow flowers on each stem. A superb cut flower and reliable perennial; leave the reedy foliage to ripen before removal.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for fragrant golden flowers in mid spring.

directions: Plant bulbs about five inches deep in a sunny, freely draining bed. Quail bears one to three deep-golden, sweetly scented flowers with slightly reflexed petals per stem. It naturalizes well and holds up in warm climates; let the slender leaves ripen before tidying.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for late-spring pink-cupped blooms.

directions: Plant bulbs about four inches deep in well-drained soil in full sun. Bell Song offers fragrant white flowers with a soft pink-rimmed cup, usually several per stem. It performs well in warmer zones and pots; keep the foliage until it dies down naturally.

planting.note: Plant in autumn near the front of a bed for late-spring flowers.

directions: Plant these tiny bulbs about three inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil. Sun Disc is a late miniature with flat, rounded golden flowers that mellow toward soft yellow. Perfect for rock gardens and troughs, it should keep its fine grassy leaves until they wither.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for tall, late-spring flowers.

directions: Plant bulbs about six inches deep in a sunny, well-drained site. Stratosphere is a tall jonquil holding two or three golden flowers with orange-toned cups on each long stem. Its height makes it a fine cut flower; let the leaves ripen fully after bloom.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in a sheltered, sunny spot.

directions: Plant bulbs about five inches deep in well-drained soil in a warm, sheltered position. Silver Chimes carries clusters of many small, fragrant flowers with white petals and pale primrose cups. It prefers milder gardens; keep the foliage until it dies back to feed the bulb.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in mild regions, or force indoors where winters are severe.

directions: Plant bulbs about five inches deep in warm, well-drained soil, or pot up for forcing in cold climates. Avalanche bears large heads of up to fifteen or more fragrant white florets with lemon-yellow cups. Provide a sheltered spot, and let the foliage ripen before removal.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in a warm, sunny position.

directions: Plant bulbs about five inches deep in freely draining soil in a sheltered, sunny spot. Falconet produces fragrant clusters of golden-yellow flowers with contrasting bright orange-red cups. Best in mild-winter areas or forced in pots; leave the foliage to ripen after bloom.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for split-cup flowers in mid to late spring.

directions: Plant bulbs about six inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil. Sorbet is a split-corona type with white petals and a frilled, flattened cup patterned in yellow and orange. Its open, ruffled face suits modern arrangements; keep the foliage until it yellows.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for early spring flowers in grass or borders.

directions: Plant bulbs about five inches deep in well-drained soil in sun or light shade. This tidy Welsh species daffodil bears neat, all-golden trumpet flowers on short, sturdy stems. Excellent for naturalizing in short grass, it should keep its foliage until it dies back on its own.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for fragrant golden flowers in mid spring.

directions: Plant bulbs about five inches deep in a sunny, freely draining bed. This old jonquil hybrid carries one to three richly fragrant, golden-yellow flowers on each rounded stem. Long-lived and tough, it naturalizes well in warm gardens; let the rush-like leaves ripen before removal.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for very early miniature blooms in a rock garden.

directions: Plant these tiny bulbs about two to three inches deep in gritty, sharply drained soil. This diminutive Spanish species is one of the earliest to flower, showing perfect little golden trumpets. Ideal for troughs and rockeries, it needs excellent drainage and should keep its short leaves until they fade.

planting.note: Set bulbs in autumn or as soon as the ground is workable in early spring.

directions: Plant bulbs about three times their own depth in loose, well-drained soil with the basal roots facing down. The white-and-raspberry upfacing flowers open in early summer on sturdy compact stems that rarely need staking. Leave foliage to die back naturally after bloom so the bulb can recharge for next year.

planting.note: Autumn planting is ideal, but spring-set bulbs still bloom the first season.

directions: Bury bulbs six to eight inches deep in fertile, fast-draining ground and water in well. Clean white upward-facing blooms appear in early to midsummer and make excellent cut flowers. Snip spent blossoms but keep the stem and leaves intact until they yellow.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring in a sunny, sharply drained bed.

directions: Set bulbs about six inches deep with a handful of grit beneath each to keep the base dry. The mahogany-red petals edged in burnt orange glow in early summer sun. Feed lightly as shoots emerge and again just before flowering.

planting.note: Best planted in autumn; spring bulbs also perform in the first year.

directions: Plant firm bulbs six inches deep in gritty, humus-rich soil. Bold pure-orange upfacing flowers open in early summer and hold their color well in full sun. Leave the ripening stem to feed the bulb, then cut it away once fully brown.

planting.note: A compact border and container lily; plant in fall or early spring.

directions: Because this dwarf grows only a foot or so tall, set bulbs four to five inches deep in pots or the front of a bed. Deep maroon-black upfacing flowers appear in early summer above short, tidy stems. Keep containers evenly moist and never let the bulbs sit in standing water.

planting.note: Plant in autumn or early spring in full sun and free-draining soil.

directions: Nestle bulbs six to eight inches deep in loose soil amended with grit. Pure white, faintly green-throated blooms face upward in early summer and are prized for cutting. Remove faded flowers to keep the display tidy but leave the leaves to feed the bulb.

planting.note: Set bulbs in fall or as soon as the soil can be worked in spring.

directions: Plant bulbs about three bulb-heights deep in gritty, fertile soil. Soft rose-pink petals shading to a cream center open upward in early to midsummer. Water during dry spells while budding, then ease off once the flowers fade.

planting.note: A short pot lily; plant in fall or early spring.

directions: Set this dwarf bulb four to five inches deep near the front of a border or in a container. Rich red upfacing flowers appear in early summer on short, wind-resistant stems. Keep the root zone cool and evenly moist during the growing season.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Plant bulbs six to eight inches deep in loose, quick-draining soil. Vivid scarlet upfacing blooms open in early summer and stand up well in the vase. Allow foliage to yellow fully before removing it so the bulb stores energy.

planting.note: Best in fall; spring bulbs bloom the same summer.

directions: Set bulbs about six inches deep in gritty, fertile soil in full sun. Golden-yellow upfacing petals marked with a deep mahogany-red center open in early summer. Deadhead spent flowers but leave the leafy stem to ripen for the season.

planting.note: Plant in autumn or early spring in humus-rich, acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant bulbs six to eight inches deep in slightly acidic soil enriched with leaf mold. Large, fragrant pink flowers with a paler edge and speckled throat open in midsummer, later than Asiatics. Give the roots shade and the tops sun, and keep soil evenly moist while budding.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring in acidic, sharply drained ground.

directions: Bury bulbs about three times their depth in rich, free-draining, lime-free soil. Deep rose-pink, strongly perfumed blooms with recurved petals appear in mid to late summer. Mulch to keep roots cool and water through dry spells until flowering finishes.

planting.note: Plant in autumn or early spring in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: Set bulbs six inches deep in humus-rich, lime-free soil with good drainage. Soft shell-pink outfacing flowers with a green-flushed throat open fragrantly in midsummer. Keep the base shaded and mulched, and avoid overhead watering on the open blooms.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring in rich, acidic, free-draining soil.

directions: Plant bulbs six to eight inches deep in leaf-mold-enriched, lime-free soil. Large, pure white fragrant flowers with slightly ruffled, recurved petals open in mid to late summer. Stake tall stems in windy sites and let foliage ripen before cutting back.

planting.note: Plant in autumn or early spring in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant bulbs about three times their height deep in rich, lime-free soil. Soft pink blushing to white flowers with a lightly spotted throat scent the garden in midsummer. Provide cool, shaded roots and steady moisture while the flower buds develop.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring in acidic, sharply drained soil.

directions: Set bulbs six inches deep in humus-rich, lime-free soil in sun with cool roots. Fragrant pink flowers with a distinctive golden-yellow central band open in midsummer. Keep evenly moist while budding and mulch lightly to conserve moisture.

planting.note: Plant in autumn or early spring in rich, acidic, free-draining soil.

directions: Plant bulbs six to eight inches deep in leaf-mold-rich, lime-free soil. Fragrant salmon-pink flowers heavily spotted with darker flecks open in mid to late summer. Shade the roots, keep moisture steady while budding, and stake tall stems if exposed.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring in deep, fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: These tall, vigorous bulbs want a deep hole eight inches down in rich, free-draining soil. Huge, lightly fragrant lemon-yellow flowers open in midsummer on stems that can top six feet. Give plenty of room, feed in spring, and stake in windy gardens.

planting.note: Plant in autumn or early spring in deep, fertile soil.

directions: Set the large bulbs eight inches deep in rich, well-drained soil with room to spread. Broad, fragrant flowers of soft apricot-yellow flushed rose open in mid to late summer. Feed as growth begins and leave stems standing until fully ripe.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring in deep, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant bulbs about eight inches deep in fertile, sharply drained soil. Creamy flowers brushed with pink and gold and edged in a picotee margin open fragrantly in midsummer. Feed in spring, water while budding, and let foliage yellow before cutting back.

planting.note: Plant in autumn or early spring in deep, fertile soil with room to grow tall.

directions: This towering Orienpet needs a deep planting hole and rich, free-draining soil to support stems that can reach seven feet. Enormous, softly fragrant pale-yellow flowers open in midsummer. Stake early, feed generously in spring, and give it plenty of elbow room.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring in deep, well-drained soil.

directions: Set bulbs about eight inches deep in fertile, quick-draining soil in full sun. Large, fragrant flowers of yellow and cream flushed rose-pink open in midsummer on strong stems. Feed as growth starts and let the foliage die down naturally each fall.

planting.note: Plant in autumn or early spring in deep, fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant these tall trumpet bulbs eight inches deep in rich, free-draining soil, tolerant of some lime. Large apricot-copper trumpet flowers with a paler throat open fragrantly in midsummer. Stake the tall stems, feed in spring, and let foliage ripen before removal.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring in deep, fertile, free-draining soil.

directions: Set bulbs about eight inches deep in rich, well-drained soil in full sun. Flat, outfacing ivory-white flowers with a bold orange central star open fragrantly in mid to late summer. Feed in spring, keep evenly moist while budding, and let the stem ripen after bloom.

planting.note: Plant in autumn or early spring in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant bulbs six to eight inches deep in rich, free-draining soil in full sun. Large upfacing flowers blend gold, apricot, and deep red into a sunset glow in early to midsummer. Deadhead spent blooms and leave the stem to ripen before cutting away.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring in fertile, sharply drained soil.

directions: Set bulbs six to eight inches deep in loose, well-drained soil in full sun. Striking white upfacing flowers are finely outlined with thin dark veins, giving the petals a penciled edge in early to midsummer. Feed lightly in spring and let foliage die back naturally.

planting.note: Best planted in early autumn; martagons resent disturbance and are slow to settle.

directions: Plant bulbs about six inches deep in humus-rich, well-drained woodland soil and leave them undisturbed to establish. Whorled stems carry many small, nodding Turk's-cap flowers of deep mahogany-red flecked with gold in early summer. Be patient the first year, as martagons often sulk before settling in.

planting.note: Plant in early autumn in a partly shaded, humus-rich bed and leave undisturbed.

directions: Set bulbs six inches deep in cool, leaf-mold-rich soil at a woodland edge. Tall whorled stems bear numerous nodding Turk's-cap flowers in soft yellow-buff flushed with rose in early summer. Mulch each autumn and disturb the roots as little as possible.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in gritty, fast-draining soil; this western species needs sharp drainage.

directions: Give this western native very sharp drainage, setting bulbs on their side in gritty soil to keep water from pooling in the scales. Fragrant white trumpet flowers that age to pink and carry fine purple dots open in midsummer. Keep it dry in summer dormancy and avoid rich, wet ground.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in gritty, well-drained soil in light woodland shade.

directions: Plant bulbs deeply in fast-draining, gritty soil at a woodland edge where summers stay dry. Tall stems carry many nodding, recurved orange Turk's-cap flowers spotted maroon in early to midsummer. Keep the resting bulb dry after the foliage fades.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in moist but well-drained, humus-rich soil near a seep or streamside.

directions: Unlike its dry-loving western cousins, this species wants consistently moist yet drained soil, as it grows wild along mountain streams. Set bulbs six inches deep in humus-rich ground with cool roots. Fragrant, clear lemon-yellow trumpet flowers open in midsummer and are lightly spotted within.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in well-drained soil; one of the earliest lilies to bloom.

directions: Plant bulbs about five inches deep in ordinary well-drained soil in sun or light shade. Nodding, strongly recurved yellow Turk's-cap flowers marked with fine dark dots open in late spring, earlier than most lilies. The blooms carry a musky scent, and the plant naturalizes readily where happy.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in deep, fertile, well-drained soil; tolerates some lime.

directions: This tough, long-lived species tolerates lime and grows well in deep, fertile soil; plant bulbs six inches deep. Many fragrant, nodding pale-yellow flowers with recurved, dark-spotted petals open in early summer. Once established it is best left undisturbed and can persist for decades.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in gritty, well-drained soil; tolerates some lime.

directions: Give this Greek native gritty, sharply drained soil in a warm, sunny spot and set bulbs about five inches deep. Waxy, brilliant sealing-wax red Turk's-cap flowers with tightly recurved petals nod from the stem in midsummer. Keep it on the dry side during summer dormancy.

planting.note: Plant in autumn or early spring in well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant the small bulbs four to five inches deep in ordinary well-drained soil in sun. Slender wiry stems carry small, star-shaped, upfacing scarlet flowers that shine in early to midsummer. This dainty species suits a rock garden or the front of a sunny border.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in cool, moist but well-drained, lime-free woodland soil.

directions: This choice Japanese species needs cool, humus-rich, lime-free soil in dappled shade; set bulbs about five inches deep. Large, fragrant, funnel-shaped flowers of clear soft pink open in early to midsummer. Keep the roots cool and moist and shelter from strong wind and midday sun.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in cool, moist, lime-free soil in light shade.

directions: Plant bulbs about four inches deep in cool, humus-rich, acidic soil with light overhead shade. Fragrant, outfacing soft rose-pink trumpet flowers open early, in late spring to early summer. Keep the roots cool and evenly moist and avoid heavy, waterlogged ground.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil.

directions: Because this stem-rooting species feeds from roots along the buried stem, plant bulbs deeply, six to eight inches down, in moist, humus-rich soil. Nodding, recurved yellow Turk's-cap flowers dotted with dark spots open in mid to late summer. Keep the soil evenly moist and mulch to cool the roots.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in consistently moist, acidic, humus-rich soil.

directions: This southern Appalachian native wants cool, constantly moist, acidic soil at a woodland or meadow edge; set bulbs about five inches deep. Small, bell-shaped, outfacing red-orange flowers speckled inside nod from the stem tips in early to midsummer. Never let its roots dry out during the growing season.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in gritty, sharply drained soil in light shade; keep dry when dormant.

directions: Give this California native fast-draining, gritty soil in dappled shade and dry summer rest; set bulbs on a bed of grit. Fragrant, upfacing flowers open white to pale lavender and deepen to rose-purple as they age in early to midsummer. Avoid rich, wet soil, which quickly rots the bulbs.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in cool, humus-rich, well-drained woodland soil.

directions: Plant bulbs about five inches deep in cool, humus-rich soil in light woodland shade. Whorled leaves surround upfacing, star-shaped orange flowers spotted with maroon that open in early summer. Keep the soil evenly moist and mulch to protect the shallow roots.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in moist but well-drained, humus-rich soil.

directions: This mountain-meadow native likes cool, constantly moist yet drained soil; plant bulbs six inches deep near a water feature or damp border. Small, bell-shaped orange-red flowers spotted maroon nod along tall stems in midsummer. Keep the roots cool and never let the soil dry out in summer.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in cool, moist, humus-rich, lime-free soil in light shade.

directions: This choice Manipur native wants cool, moist, acidic soil and dappled shade; set bulbs about four inches deep. Nodding, bell-shaped flowers of soft pink flushed purple outside open in early summer. Shelter from strong sun and wind and keep the root zone consistently cool and moist.

planting.note: Plant in autumn or early spring in moist, well-drained soil; extremely cold-hardy.

directions: One of the hardiest of all lilies, this species thrives in cold climates; plant bulbs about five inches deep in moist, well-drained soil. Broad, upfacing red-orange flowers spotted with dark flecks open in early summer, often several per stem. An ancestor of many Asiatic hybrids, it naturalizes well in a sunny border.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in cool, moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in light shade.

directions: This stoloniferous species wanders underground, so plant bulbs six inches deep in cool, moist, humus-rich soil and give it room to roam. Fragrant, nodding white Turk's-cap flowers marbled with purple flecks open in midsummer. Keep the roots cool and moist and let the colony spread naturally.

planting.note: Set out after the ground thaws and hard frost has passed in early spring.

directions: Plant the crown level with the soil surface so the growing point is never buried, spacing plants about a foot apart in humus-rich, well-drained ground. The ruffled amber-gold foliage colors best in bright shade with a little morning sun. Airy pink flower spikes rise above the mound in late spring, and lifting and dividing every three years keeps the clump vigorous.

planting.note: Transplant in early spring once soil can be worked.

directions: Dig a hole slightly wider than the root ball and settle the plant with its crown at grade in loose, organic soil. The purple leaves are veiled with silver and hold their color through the season in dappled shade. Slender stems of tiny white flowers appear in early summer; shear spent stalks to tidy the mound.

planting.note: Plant out in spring after the last hard freeze.

directions: Work compost into the bed and set the plant so the crown sits just above the surrounding soil to avoid rot. Coffee-brown foliage warms to caramel tones where a bit more light reaches it. Cream-colored flower wands rise in late spring, and a springtime mulch helps hold the shallow roots in place.

planting.note: Set out in early spring as growth resumes.

directions: Plant into fertile, well-drained soil with the crown at grade and firm the roots gently. The bronze-red leaves take on deeper cherry tones in cooler weather. Showy coral-red bell flowers cover tall stems in early summer and draw hummingbirds; deadhead to prolong the display.

planting.note: Transplant in spring once the soil warms.

directions: Give this vigorous grower a foot and a half of space and plant with the crown level in rich, moisture-retentive soil. The large ruffled leaves are chocolate above and burgundy beneath, rippling in the breeze. Tall airy stems of small cream flowers rise in early summer above the bold mound.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in a shaded, sheltered spot.

directions: Settle the plant with its crown at soil level in humus-rich ground that drains freely. Mint-green leaves overlaid with silver frosting and green veining light up a shady border. Fine cream flower spikes appear in early summer, and a yearly topdress of compost keeps the crown from heaving.

planting.note: Set out in spring after frost danger eases.

directions: Plant the crown at grade in well-drained soil and mulch lightly to conserve moisture. The compact leaves emerge peachy-orange with silver veining and mature to rusty amber. Slim flower stems appear in late spring; a spot with morning sun deepens the warm foliage color.

planting.note: Transplant in early spring into moist, rich soil.

directions: Dig in plenty of organic matter and plant with the crown resting at the soil line. The heavily ruffled leaves are glossy purple above with rosy-red undersides that flash as they curl. Creamy flower spikes rise in early summer; divide crowded clumps in spring to renew them.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this villosa type tolerates heat and humidity well.

directions: Set the crown at grade in well-drained, humus-rich soil; its hairy villosa parentage makes it more forgiving of Southern heat. Silvery leaves form a sturdy mound that resists summer decline. Tall stems carry a long-lasting show of rose-pink bell flowers from midsummer into fall.

planting.note: Set out in spring; tolerates more sun than fancy-leaf types.

directions: Plant the crown level with the soil in fertile, well-drained ground and keep evenly moist while establishing. Grown mainly for bloom, it forms neat green mounds topped with wiry stems of vivid ruby-red bells. Flowering runs from late spring into summer and repeats if spent stalks are removed; this is a favorite of hummingbirds.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into rich, moist but drained soil.

directions: Plant with the crown at grade and give it bright, indirect light to keep the variegation crisp. Green leaves are boldly mottled and splashed with creamy white for season-long interest. Coral-pink flower spikes rise in late spring, and removing all-green shoots that revert keeps the marbled look.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in a partly shaded bed.

directions: Allow generous room and plant with the crown at the soil line in loose, organic ground. Large ruffled leaves blend smoky purple, silver, and slate gray like a squall over water. Airy cream flowers rise in early summer; refresh the clump by dividing every few springs.

planting.note: Set out in spring for its shifting seasonal foliage colors.

directions: Plant the crown at grade in fertile, well-drained soil and keep lightly moist. The chartreuse leaves are flushed with red veining and shift from lime to amber and rose across the seasons. Short cream flower stems appear in early summer; the low mound is well suited to the front of a shady border or a container.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into humus-rich soil.

directions: Plant with the crown level and mulch to keep the roots cool and moist. Large silvery leaves are laced with charcoal veins and hold their sheen through the growing season. Creamy white flowers rise on slim stems in early summer over the robust mound.

planting.note: Plant in spring after hard frosts have passed.

directions: Set the plant with its crown at grade in rich, well-drained soil and water in thoroughly. Fiery red-orange young leaves mature toward bronze, glowing where morning sun reaches them. Small red-tinged flowers appear on wiry stems in early summer above the mounded foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this americana selection handles heat and humidity.

directions: Plant the crown at grade in fertile, well-drained soil enriched with compost. Selected from a native species, it forms a heat-tolerant mound of deep maroon-black leaves. Airy spikes of tiny cream flowers rise in early summer, and the sturdy clumps hold up well in warm regions.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant with the crown at the soil surface in humus-rich ground and mulch lightly. The glossy purple-black leaves have a silver overlay that keeps the color rich all season. Slim stems of white flowers rise in early summer, and the tidy mound suits borders and containers alike.

planting.note: Set out in spring in a partly shaded, well-drained spot.

directions: Plant the crown level with the soil in rich, freely draining ground and keep evenly moist. The ruffled leaves are deep cherry-red above with rosy undersides that show as they curl. Airy white flowers rise on slender stems in early summer over the compact mound.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this large-leaved selection is vigorous and heat-tolerant.

directions: Give this wide grower up to two feet of space and plant with the crown at grade in rich, well-drained soil. The big glossy leaves are nearly black with ruffled, scalloped edges. Pinkish-white flowers appear in early summer, and the robust mound makes a bold groundcover.

planting.note: Transplant in spring for its color-shifting foliage.

directions: Plant with the crown at soil level in fertile, well-drained ground and mulch to hold moisture. The large leaves emerge peachy-amber with a silver veil and mature to rosy burgundy tones. Creamy flowers rise in early summer above the wide, mounding foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this series is bred for extra winter hardiness.

directions: Plant the crown at grade in well-drained soil; this cold-tough selection resists frost heaving better than most. Bright chartreuse-lime leaves brighten shade and glow with a bit of morning sun. Cream flowers appear on short stems in early summer above the durable mound.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter and transplant after frost, or set out nursery plants in spring.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed and press it in without covering, keeping it lightly moist until seedlings appear. Native to the California Channel Islands, this large species forms bold clumps of rounded green leaves and tolerates dry shade once established. Tall stems of pale pink-white flowers rise in late winter and spring; water sparingly through summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter; this alpine species benefits from cold stratification.

directions: Chill the seed for a few weeks, then surface-sow and keep cool and moist until it sprouts. A compact Western species of rocky slopes, it forms small tufts of scalloped leaves and thrives in sharp-draining, gritty soil. Wiry stems carry pink-tinged flowers in late spring; it makes a fine rock-garden or trough plant.

planting.note: Start seed indoors after cold stratification and set out in spring.

directions: Cold-stratify the seed, then surface-sow and keep barely moist until germination. Endemic to New Mexico's Sandia Mountains, this dwarf species forms tidy little mounds of small rounded leaves. Short stems bear fragrant pink flowers in early summer, making it a prized alpine and trough subject in gritty, well-drained soil.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter; prefers cool, moist conditions.

directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed and keep it evenly cool and moist until seedlings emerge. A smooth-leaved species of Pacific Northwest streambanks and rocky slopes, it likes moist, gritty soil in dappled shade. Delicate airy sprays of small white flowers appear in early summer above the glossy foliage.

planting.note: Start seed indoors after a cold period and transplant in spring.

directions: Cold-stratify and surface-sow the seed, keeping it lightly moist until it sprouts. Named for its gooseberry-like lobed leaves, this Rocky Mountain species forms compact rosettes in rocky, well-drained ground. Slender stems of small white flowers rise in early summer; it suits dry rock gardens.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter and set out in spring.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed and keep it cool and moist until germination. A robust Pacific Northwest native, it forms large clumps of broad, lobed leaves in meadows and open woods. Tall stout stems carry dense spikes of small greenish flowers in early summer.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant in spring into woodland soil.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it moist and lightly shaded until seedlings appear. A Southeastern U.S. woodland native, it forms mounds of rounded, lobed green leaves in rich, humusy soil. Tall airy stems bear long, narrow greenish-cream bells in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Start seed indoors or set out nursery plants in spring.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed and keep it lightly moist until it germinates. A hairy-leaved California coastal species, it forms fuzzy green mounds that tolerate salt spray and dry shade. Short stout stems bear fluffy pink-white flower spikes in spring; water sparingly once established.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade with rich, moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant the crown at grade in humus-rich woodland soil and keep it consistently moist. The deeply cut green leaves are marked with dark central blotches for season-long interest. Foamy spikes of soft pink buds open to white flowers in mid to late spring; shear spent stalks to tidy the clump.

planting.note: Set out in spring in a shaded, evenly moist bed.

directions: Plant with the crown at the soil surface in rich, moisture-retentive ground and mulch with leaf mold. This selection is prized for green leaves finely speckled and misted with creamy white variegation. Airy white flower spikes rise in mid-spring above the marbled foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this running type spreads by stolons.

directions: Plant the crown at grade and give it room to run, since it spreads by creeping stems to form a groundcover. The lobed green leaves carry dark central markings and take on red tints in fall. Spikes of foamy white flowers appear in mid-spring above the trailing mat.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into shaded, humus-rich soil.

directions: Plant with the crown at the soil surface in cool, moist woodland soil and mulch to conserve moisture. The narrow, deeply dissected leaves resemble old skeleton keys and are marked with dark centers. Slender spikes of white flowers appear in mid to late spring above the distinctive foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring; spreads by runners to cover ground in shade.

directions: Set the crown at grade and space plants to let the stolons knit into a groundcover. The deeply lobed green leaves have dark central blotches and spread on running stems. Foamy white flower spikes rise in mid-spring, making it useful for shady banks and woodland edges.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into a partly shaded, well-drained spot.

directions: Plant the crown level with the soil in rich, freely draining ground and keep evenly moist. Golden-yellow leaves are centered with a deep red blotch, and this selection also blooms well. Rosy-pink flowers appear on short stems in late spring above the glowing foliage.

planting.note: Transplant in spring for its bold seasonal color change.

directions: Plant the crown at grade in rich, well-drained soil and mulch to hold moisture. The maple-shaped leaves are golden-green with a dark center in spring and blaze to fiery orange-red in fall. White flowers rise on short stems in late spring above the color-shifting mound.

planting.note: Set out in spring in a partly shaded, well-drained bed.

directions: Plant with the crown at soil level in rich, freely draining ground and keep evenly moist. The rounded leaves emerge honey-amber and mature toward rosy tones with darker veining. Pink-blushed white flowers rise on short stems in late spring above the compact mound.

planting.note: Plant in spring in bright shade to keep the lime color strong.

directions: Plant the crown at grade in humus-rich, well-drained soil and mulch to keep roots cool. The bright lime-green leaves carry a red blush at the center along the veins. Airy white flowers appear on short stems in late spring above the glowing chartreuse mound.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and move seedlings out after the last spring frost.

directions: Sow seed just below the surface and keep evenly moist until sprouts appear. Set plants in well-drained soil where they get all-day sun, and give them room for good airflow. Petals open lime-green and blush pink toward the tips as the flowers age. Leave spent cones standing to feed finches through fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions or nursery starts in spring once the soil has warmed.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil surface in a sunny, well-drained bed. The wide, horizontally held white rays carry a light honey scent, so place it near a path where you will notice it. Water through the first season to establish, then it tolerates dry spells. Deadhead early blooms to extend flowering or leave seed heads for birds.

planting.note: Plant container-grown stock in spring after frost danger passes.

directions: Give this cultivar lean, sharply drained soil and full sun for the sturdiest stems. Flowers open scarlet-red with a dark cone and horizontally poised petals on strong branching stems. Avoid rich or soggy ground, which shortens the plant's life. Cut a few stems for the vase or leave cones for winter interest.

planting.note: Set out plants in spring into warm, workable soil.

directions: Plant in full sun with the crown at grade in soil that drains freely. The pom-pom style double centers open coral and soften as they mature, held on tall upright stems. Keep young plants watered until roots take hold, then ease off. Shear spent flowers to keep new buds coming through late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring so roots establish before summer heat.

directions: Choose a sunny spot with average, well-drained soil and space plants for airflow. Blooms shift through tangerine, rose, and pink tones with a coppery cone, so a single clump reads as several colors at once. Water regularly the first year, then only in drought. Deadhead to prolong the show or leave heads to self-sow lightly.

planting.note: Plant in spring into full sun and well-drained ground.

directions: Set plants in fast-draining soil where they bake in full sun. Fragrant rich orange rays surround a red-brown cone and hold color as the blooms age. Keep the crown from sitting wet over winter to improve survival. Snip a few stems for cutting or leave the cones standing for seed-eating birds.

planting.note: Plant out in spring after the soil warms.

directions: Plant in a sunny border with sharp drainage and average fertility. Warm salmon-orange rays deepen to rosy tones near the dark cone and carry a soft fragrance. Establish with regular water, then treat it as drought tolerant. Remove faded flowers to encourage a longer bloom period.

planting.note: Plant in spring into a warm, well-drained bed.

directions: Site in full sun with free-draining soil for the best flowering. Rose-pink rays radiate from a deep red central cone on compact, well-branched stems. Water new plants through their first summer, then only during dry stretches. Deadheading keeps blooms coming, though the cones also draw goldfinches.

planting.note: Plant compact starts in spring once frost has passed.

directions: This dwarf selection suits the front of a border or a large container in full sun. Magenta rays glow against near-black stems and a dark cone, and the scent is sweet. Use gritty, well-drained soil and avoid winter wet. Trim off spent heads to tidy the plant and prompt reblooming.

planting.note: Plant in spring into full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: Give this tall selection full sun and soil that never stays soggy. Large, glowing pumpkin-orange rays sit on thick upright stems and hold their intensity well. Water to establish, then it handles heat and drought. Leave a few seed heads for winter structure and birds.

planting.note: Set out in spring into a hot, sunny, well-drained site.

directions: Plant in lean, fast-draining soil in the sunniest spot you have. Deep red rays surround a near-black cone on dark, sturdy stems that resist flopping. Keep water modest once established to promote longevity. Deadhead for a longer display or let the last cones ripen for seed.

planting.note: Plant in spring so it can settle before flowering.

directions: Set plants in full sun and average, well-drained soil with room to spread. Fully double, fluffy pink pom-pom flowers top strong stems and last well when cut. Water through establishment, then during dry spells only. Remove spent blooms to keep the plant producing fresh flowers.

planting.note: Plant in spring into full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: Plant with the crown level in fertile, freely draining soil in full sun. Rounded double centers in soft melon-orange sit above a collar of matching rays. Keep evenly watered while establishing, then reduce. Shear off finished flowers to encourage a steady succession of blooms.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost into full sun.

directions: Give this compact double full sun and soil that drains well. Puffy raspberry-pink centers rise above the same-colored rays on tidy, well-branched plants. Water regularly until rooted in, then ease off. Deadhead to prolong flowering into late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring into a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Plant in full sun with good drainage for the strongest bloom. Double blooms open in a blend of orange and berry tones on compact, branching stems. Provide steady water the first season, then only when dry. Clip spent flowers to keep the plant looking fresh and blooming.

planting.note: Plant dwarf starts in spring at the front of a border.

directions: This dwarf, double coneflower fits small spaces and pots in full sun. Pink pom-pom centers sit over a skirt of rose rays on a compact, rounded plant. Use well-drained soil and water while it establishes. Deadhead to keep it neat and flowering over a long season.

planting.note: Plant in spring into full sun and gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this compact, prairie-derived selection in full sun with sharp drainage. Rosy-pink single flowers cover a dense, dwarf mound that blooms freely. It is tough and drought tolerant once its roots are down. Leave the small cones standing to feed birds and add winter interest.

planting.note: Plant in spring once the soil warms, in full sun.

directions: Site in full sun with lean, well-drained soil for the sturdiest habit. Warm mango-yellow rays surround a golden cone and carry a light honey scent. This prairie hybrid is heat and drought tolerant after establishment. Deadhead for tidiness or leave cones for goldfinches.

planting.note: Plant in spring into full sun and well-drained ground.

directions: Plant in a sunny bed with average, free-draining soil. Soft pineapple-yellow flowers age to creamy tones on strong, upright stems. Keep watered until established, then reduce watering. Remove faded blooms to extend the flowering season.

planting.note: Plant in spring into a hot, sunny, well-drained site.

directions: Give this cultivar full sun and soil that drains freely. Rays open bright yellow-orange and deepen to red as the flowers age, so a single plant shows several shades at once. Water to establish, then it takes heat well. Deadhead to keep new blooms opening through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring into full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: Plant in a sunny border with sharp drainage and average soil. Fragrant flowers blend peach and rose around a bright orange cone on branching stems. Water new plants regularly, then only in drought. Snip finished flowers to prolong bloom or leave cones for the birds.

planting.note: Plant compact starts in spring in full sun.

directions: This compact selection suits pots and border fronts in full sun. Bright golden-yellow rays surround a golden cone on a dense, rounded plant that blooms heavily. Use well-drained soil and water while establishing. Deadhead to keep the tidy mound flowering all season.

planting.note: Plant in spring into full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this compact, sturdy-stemmed selection in full sun with good drainage. Deep orange rays sit on strong, well-branched stems that hold up without staking. Water through the first season, then it tolerates heat and dry spells. Deadhead for a longer show or leave cones for winter birds.

planting.note: Plant in spring into a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Give this compact coneflower full sun and freely draining soil. Rich red rays cover the plant on tidy, branching stems that resist flopping. Water to establish, then reduce as it becomes drought tolerant. Clip spent flowers to keep new buds forming.

planting.note: Plant in spring into full sun and well-drained ground.

directions: Plant this compact selection in full sun where drainage is good. Golden-yellow rays sit on sturdy, well-branched stems on a neat, rounded plant. Provide regular water while establishing, then only in drought. Deadhead to extend flowering into late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun toward the front of a bed.

directions: This compact white sport suits small gardens and border edges in full sun. Creamy-white drooping rays surround a coppery cone on a dwarf, bushy plant. Use well-drained soil and water through establishment. Deadhead to prolong bloom or leave cones for seed-eating birds.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and transplant out after the last frost.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and keep the surface moist until germination. This novelty produces a second tuft of small pink petals on top of the cone, often more freely in the second season. Plant in full sun with well-drained soil and average water. Deadhead to encourage more of the curious double-tiered flowers.

planting.note: Plant in spring into full sun or light afternoon shade.

directions: Plant this variegated selection where its cream-edged foliage catches the eye. Rosy-pink flowers rise above leaves margined in white on a compact plant. Give it well-drained soil and some afternoon shade in hot climates to protect the variegation. Keep evenly watered while established and remove spent blooms to extend flowering.

planting.note: Plant in spring into full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: Plant in full sun with soil that drains well. Fragrant, deep rosy-purple rays surround a dark cone on well-branched, upright stems. Water to establish, then treat it as drought tolerant. Deadhead to keep it blooming and to tidy the plant.

planting.note: Plant compact starts in spring in full sun.

directions: This dwarf double suits containers and border fronts in full sun. Fluffy white pom-pom centers sit over a collar of white rays on a compact plant. Use well-drained soil and water while roots establish. Deadhead to keep the neat mound producing fresh flowers.

planting.note: Plant in spring into full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: Plant in full sun with average, free-draining soil. Creamy-white, densely double pom-pom flowers top strong upright stems and last well in the vase. Water through the first season, then reduce. Deadhead to prolong the display through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring into full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: Give this tall double full sun and soil that drains freely. Large magenta-pink pom-pom flowers sit on strong stems that are excellent for cutting. Water to establish, then during dry stretches only. Remove spent blooms to keep the plant flowering.

planting.note: Plant in spring into full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: Plant in full sun with good drainage and average soil. Double flowers open with a greenish tint and mature to a creamy white pom-pom over pale rays. Water through establishment, then only in drought. Deadhead to prolong the flowering season.

planting.note: Plant in spring into full sun and well-drained ground.

directions: Plant in full sun with room for its large flowers and sturdy stems. Very big rosy-red blooms with nearly horizontal rays surround a dark cone and draw butterflies. Water to establish, then it handles heat and dry spells. Leave the big cones standing to feed birds and add winter form.

planting.note: Plant in spring into full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this compact, well-branched selection in full sun with good drainage. Deep red rays cover a dense, mounded plant that blooms early and heavily. Water while establishing, then reduce as it becomes drought tolerant. Deadhead to keep new flowers opening across the season.

planting.note: Plant in spring into a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Give this compact coneflower full sun and freely draining soil. Rich raspberry-pink rays blanket a tidy, well-branched mound that flowers early. Water through the first season, then only in drought. Clip spent flowers to prolong bloom and keep the plant neat.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed, start indoors, and transplant after the last frost.

directions: Give seed a month of cold, moist stratification before sowing shallowly. This limestone-glade native carries slender pale-pink to lavender rays over a dark cone on wiry stems. Plant in full sun with lean, sharply drained soil, where it is very drought tolerant. Leave the cones to ripen seed for birds and natural reseeding.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed, start indoors, and set out after frost.

directions: Cold-stratify the seed for several weeks, then sow shallowly and keep lightly moist. This tough prairie native holds short reddish-purple rays around a dark, spiny cone on slender stems. Plant in full sun with well-drained soil, and it tolerates drought and poor ground once established. Leave the seed heads standing for finches and winter interest.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring or early summer for the deepest color the following season.

directions: Press the rosettes firmly into lean, sharply drained ground with the crown level to the soil line. The wine-red rosettes with green heart color up strongest in bright sun and cool nights, so avoid rich soil and shade that mute the tones. Give it a poor gravel or sand mulch, withhold fertilizer, and lift a few offsets each year to share or fill gaps.

planting.note: Plant in full sun so the near-black centers develop fully.

directions: Plant the rosettes into sharp, gritty soil with the base level to the surface and firm lightly. Deep maroon-black rosette centers contrast with green outer leaves, darkening most under strong sun and lean conditions. Keep the bed dry and unfed, and lift crowded offsets each year to maintain the striking dark color.

planting.note: Best in alkaline, gritty soil in full sun.

directions: Set the rosettes into a sharply drained, lime-tolerant gravel mix at soil level. This classic form bears large blue-green rosettes whose leaf tips are dipped in rich chestnut-red, forming handsome geometric clusters. Water minimally, avoid rich feeding, and divide offsets to keep rosettes well shaped.

planting.note: Plant in full sun and gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: Press the rosettes into a fast-draining, gravelly bed with the crown at the surface. It forms symmetrical grey-green rosettes with subtle reddish tips, offsetting freely into neat colonies ideal for troughs and crevices. Keep it lean and dry, and replant surplus chicks each spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and average, well-drained soil.

directions: Set divisions into well-drained soil with the crown at the surface and firm around the roots. This low, spreading mat produces masses of golden-yellow star flowers in early summer that fade to orange-red seed heads, with foliage bronzing in fall. Water occasionally until established, then let it thrive on neglect and shear spent flowers if desired.

planting.note: Full sun brings out the darkest mahogany foliage.

directions: Press divisions or cuttings into gritty, well-drained soil so the stems make contact and root quickly. Deep mahogany-red foliage forms a dense creeping carpet topped with rosy-red star flowers in summer. Give it full sun and lean soil for the best color, and shear lightly after bloom to keep it tidy.

planting.note: Plant in full sun for the richest red winter tones.

directions: Lay divisions on well-drained soil and firm them so the trailing stems root along their length. The scalloped foliage flushes red in sun and cold and is topped with deep pink-red flowers in summer, forming a solid low mat. Water sparingly once established and clip back overgrown edges to keep it dense.

planting.note: Plant in full sun and sharply drained soil.

directions: Scatter or press short cuttings onto gritty, fast-draining soil where they root readily along the stems. The tiny cylindrical leaves take on bronze-red tones in sun, and clouds of white star flowers appear in summer over the low mat. It tolerates heat, drought, and poor soil, so water only during extended dry spells.

planting.note: Plant in full sun; tolerates very poor, dry soil.

directions: Press cuttings into gritty soil where they root quickly to form a dense green carpet. Named for its spirally arranged leaves and mild taste compared with peppery goldmoss, it produces bright yellow star flowers in early to mid summer. It thrives in heat and drought, so water rarely and shear off spent blooms if you prefer.

planting.note: Evergreen groundcover for full sun to light shade.

directions: Plant divisions into well-drained soil and firm so the creeping stems knit into an even mat. The glossy green scalloped foliage stays largely evergreen, taking on reddish winter tints, and yellow star flowers appear in summer. It makes a tough groundcover and green-roof plant, needing only occasional water once established.

planting.note: Full sun deepens the chocolate-brown foliage color.

directions: Root short cuttings in gritty, well-drained soil in a sunny spot. The needle-like foliage turns rich chocolate-brown in strong sun, greening in shade, and forms mounded rounded clumps topped with yellow star flowers. Keep it lean and dry, and pinch or trim to maintain the compact, ball-like shape.
